title: Rewrite my rough draft into a clear, friendly message (keep my meaning)
content:
Rewrite the text below into a clear, friendly message that keeps my original meaning and key details. Don’t add new facts. If something is ambiguous, keep it neutral rather than guessing.

Requirements:
- Keep it concise and easy to skim
- Preserve any dates, numbers, names, and action items exactly
- Use a warm, helpful tone (not overly formal)
- If the message includes requests, make them explicit and polite
- Fix grammar, tighten wording, remove repetition

Output:
1) Improved version
2) 3 alternative subject lines (if it’s an email/message with a subject)
3) A short list of any unclear points you noticed (as questions I could answer)

Text to rewrite:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Turn product features into benefit-led landing page copy (fast, structured)
content:
Write landing page copy for the product described below. Keep the tone confident and structured. Don’t be fluffy; be specific and customer-focused.

Product info (paste and fill in):
- Product name:
- Target customer (role + context):
- Primary problem it solves:
- 3–6 key features (bullets):
- Differentiator vs alternatives:
- Proof (numbers, testimonials, logos, case study, or “none yet”):
- Desired action (CTA):
- Brand voice constraints (words to use/avoid):

Output exactly these sections:
1) Hero
- Headline (max 10 words)
- Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
- Primary CTA + secondary CTA

2) “Why this” value props
- 3 benefit-led bullets (each starts with a strong verb)

3) How it works
- 3 steps, one short sentence each

4) Key features → benefits
- A two-column list: Feature | Customer benefit (5 rows)

5) Social proof
- If proof exists: 2 short proof bullets + 1 testimonial (2–3 sentences)
- If none: write 3 credibility builders (e.g., process, guarantees, founder expertise) without pretending you have metrics

6) Objections handled
- 4 common objections with crisp rebuttals (1–2 sentences each)

7) Final CTA block
- 1 closing paragraph (max 60 words)
- Repeat primary CTA

Rules:
- Write for a scanning reader: short lines, no long paragraphs.
- Avoid jargon unless the target customer uses it daily.
- If the inputs are vague, make 3 reasonable assumptions and list them at the top before writing the copy.

title: Cinematic dark-mode social post: product-in-hand hero shot
content:
Create a square (1:1) social media hero image with a premium dark-mode aesthetic. Subject: a hand holding a sleek matte-black smartphone at a slight angle, screen on, showing an abstract UI (no readable text), with subtle blue/purple gradient glow reflecting on the fingers. Setting: minimal studio scene with a deep charcoal background and faint haze for depth.

Composition: tight crop, rule-of-thirds, phone centered slightly right, shallow depth of field, bokeh falloff, negative space on the left for optional overlay. Lighting: low-key, single soft key light from upper left + dim rim light from behind to separate edges; soft shadows, controlled specular highlights on the phone frame.

Style: modern, clean, restrained saturation, cool/neutral color grading, cinematic contrast, high-end tech advertising feel. Camera: 50mm, f/1.8, ISO 200, 1/125. Render as photorealistic. Avoid: bright daylight, warm cozy tones, cluttered backgrounds, visible brand logos, readable UI text, exaggerated neon, lens distortion.

title: Write a clear decision-request email that gets a fast yes/no
content:
Help me draft a concise decision-request email.

Context (paste and keep it brief):
- Recipient role + relationship to me:
- Topic/decision needed:
- Why it matters / impact:
- Options (A/B/C) with 1–2 pros/cons each:
- My recommendation:
- Deadline / when we need the answer:
- Constraints (budget, timeline, policy, dependencies):
- Links or references to include:

Requirements:
- Tone: clear, practical, respectful, not salesy.
- Length: 120–180 words.
- Make the decision request explicit and easy to answer.
- Use short paragraphs and, if helpful, a compact bullet list for options.
- Include a specific call to action with a deadline.
- Avoid jargon and vague language.

Deliver:
1) Subject line options (5), optimized for quick open + clarity.
2) The email body.
3) A one-line “If you’re short on time” version.

Output only the subject lines and email text (no explanations).

title: Turn a messy meeting transcript into an actionable decision log + owners
content:
Convert the text below into a crisp decision log and action plan for a business team.

Input:
[PASTE MEETING NOTES / TRANSCRIPT]

Output (use exactly these sections):
1) Decisions made (bullet list)
- Decision:
- Rationale (1 line):
- Date/meeting:

2) Action items (table)
Columns: Task | Owner | Due date | Priority (H/M/L) | Dependencies | Success criteria

3) Open questions / unresolved items
- Question:
- Who will answer:
- By when:

4) Risks & blockers
- Risk/blocker:
- Impact:
- Mitigation:
- Owner:

Rules:
- If an owner or due date isn’t stated, infer a reasonable placeholder (e.g., “TBD—Project Lead”, “TBD—next weekly sync”) and flag it with “(TBD)”.
- Merge duplicates, remove chatter, and keep wording businesslike.
- Do not invent facts; only infer structure and missing fields.
- Keep the whole output under 350 words unless the input is extremely long.

title: Quick UX critique + 5 actionable fixes for a screen or flow
content:
Act like a practical UX designer. I’ll paste a screen description, wireframe notes, or a flow. Give me a quick critique and concrete improvements.

Input:
- Product + audience:
- Goal of the screen/flow:
- Context (mobile/web, logged in/out, constraints):
- Current copy (if any):
- What I’m worried about:

Output (keep it concise and friendly):
1) One-sentence verdict (what’s working / what’s not)
2) The top 5 issues (ranked by user impact) — each with: why it’s a problem + a specific fix
3) Revised structure: suggested layout/sections or step-by-step flow (bulleted)
4) Copy improvements: rewrite the primary CTA, one key headline, and any error/helper text you think is missing
5) 3 quick questions I should answer to finalize the design

Assume standard UX best practices, avoid jargon, and prioritize changes that are easy to implement.

title: Cinematic Product Shot Prompt Builder (Dark UI-Friendly)
content:
Create an image-generation prompt for a premium, dark-interface-friendly scene.

Subject: [what the image is of]
Style: cinematic, modern, restrained, high-end (no cheerful stock-photo vibe)
Setting: minimal, subdued background in deep gray/charcoal/navy; subtle texture allowed (concrete, matte metal, smoked glass)
Lighting: low-key, directional key light + soft fill; controlled highlights; soft shadows; gentle falloff; no harsh sunlight
Color grade: cool/neutral, slightly desaturated, clean contrast; avoid warm/orange casts
Composition: clear focal point, negative space for UI overlay, shallow-to-medium depth of field; avoid clutter
Camera: [lens mm] prime look, f/[aperture], ISO [value], 1/[shutter]s; [angle: eye-level / 3-4 view / top-down]
Materials detail: call out finishes (matte, anodized aluminum, frosted plastic, polished glass) and micro-texture
Quality constraints: sharp subject edges, realistic reflections, no text, no logos, no extra objects unless specified

Output ONLY a single finished prompt with concrete specifics. Ask no questions—make reasonable assumptions.

Example input fields:
Subject: compact wireless earbud case
Lens: 85mm
Aperture: f/2.8
Angle: 3-4 view

Now generate the final image prompt.

title: Cinematic developer desk with code reflections in a dark UI aesthetic
content:
Moody, cinematic close-up of a modern developer workstation at night: a matte-black desk with a low-profile mechanical keyboard, dark trackpad, and a single ultrawide monitor showing a code editor (legible-looking syntax but no brand logos). Subtle reflections of teal/blue code on the desk surface. Add a ceramic mug, a small notebook with minimal linework, and one slim desk lamp casting a narrow, cool-white beam.

Composition: 3/4 angle from slightly above desk height, subject centered on the monitor + keyboard, shallow depth of field (keyboard in crisp focus, background softly falling off). Background: subdued dark studio environment with faint bokeh from a distant LED strip, no bright windows.

Lighting & color: low-key directional lighting, soft shadows, controlled contrast, cool/neutral grade (deep navy, charcoal, cool gray, restrained teal highlights), no warm tungsten cast. Premium, minimal, high-end product-interface feel.

Style: photorealistic, clean, modern, no clutter, no people, no visible brand marks, no on-screen UI watermarks. Camera look: 50mm equivalent, f/2.0, ISO 400, subtle film grain, slight vignette.

title: Turn a messy to-do list into a 30-minute action plan
content:
I’m going to paste a messy to-do list. Convert it into a focused 30-minute action plan I can start right now.

Rules:
- Only include tasks I can realistically start and make progress on in the next 30 minutes.
- Break each chosen task into tiny steps (2–6 minutes each).
- If something is blocked, replace it with the quickest unblock step.
- Keep it strict: maximum 5 tasks total.
- Use plain language and be specific about what I should click/type/do.

Output format:
1) Top goal for the next 30 minutes (one sentence)
2) 30-minute timeline (00:00–30:00) with minute ranges and micro-steps
3) “If I get stuck” fallback list (3 quick options)
4) What to prep before starting (max 3 items)

Here’s my to-do list:
[PASTE LIST HERE]

title: Evidence-First Research Brief with Source Quality Scoring and Open Questions
content:
Act as my research analyst. I’m going to give you a topic and (optionally) a few links or notes.

Deliver an evidence-first research brief that I can trust. Keep it professional and concise, but don’t skip rigor.

Input (I will fill in):
- Research question / decision to support:
- Context (industry, geography, timeframe, audience):
- What I already believe (so you can challenge it):
- Constraints (budget, tools, time, risk tolerance):
- Any sources/links/notes:

Output structure:
1) Bottom-line answer (5–8 bullets)
   - Each bullet must include: confidence (High/Med/Low) + what it depends on.

2) Key findings (grouped by theme)
   - For each finding: claim → evidence summary → citations.
   - Highlight whether evidence is causal, correlational, or expert opinion.

3) Source table
   - Columns: Source | Type (peer-reviewed / gov / industry / news / blog / dataset) | Date | Relevance | Method notes | Limitations | Credibility score (0–5) | Link/citation.
   - Scoring rubric:
     - 5 = primary data, transparent methods, reputable publisher, recent, directly relevant.
     - 3 = plausible secondary synthesis or older/partial primary data.
     - 1 = anecdotal, unclear methods, marketing-driven, or outdated.

4) What’s uncertain / disputed
   - List the top disagreements in the literature or industry.
   - Explain why they disagree (definitions, measurement, incentives, sample bias, etc.).

5) Decision implications
   - If the goal is to choose between options, present a comparison table with criteria and tradeoffs.
   - Include a “what would change my mind” section: 3–5 critical facts to verify.

6) Research gaps and next steps
   - The smallest set of additional checks to increase confidence quickly.
   - Include suggested search queries, datasets, and who to interview.

Rules:
- Do not invent citations. If you can’t verify something, label it as “unverified” and suggest how to verify.
- Prefer primary sources and data; de-emphasize press releases and SEO blogs.
- Use crisp bullets, not long paragraphs.
- If my question is ambiguous, ask up to 3 clarifying questions first; otherwise proceed.

Topic to research: [PASTE HERE]

title: Write a One-Page Business Proposal (Clear Scope, Timeline, Pricing Options, Next Steps)
content:
Help me write a one-page business proposal that I can paste into an email or PDF. Keep it friendly, helpful, and easy to skim. Use short paragraphs and bullets where useful.

Inputs (fill in what you can):
- Client/company:
- Their goal/problem:
- My offer/service:
- Key outcomes (3–5 bullets):
- Scope (what’s included):
- Out of scope (what’s not included):
- Timeline:
- Requirements from client:
- Pricing (include 2 options if possible):
- Payment terms:
- Assumptions/risks:
- Call to action (meeting link/date range):
- My credibility (1–2 lines, optional):

Output format:
1) Title + one-sentence summary
2) Client goal (1 short paragraph)
3) Proposed approach (3–6 bullets)
4) Deliverables (bulleted list)
5) Timeline (simple week-by-week or milestone list)
6) Pricing (Option A / Option B with what changes)
7) What I need from you (bullets)
8) Next steps (single clear ask)

Constraints:
- No buzzwords or vague claims.
- Make deliverables measurable where possible.
- Keep it to ~300–450 words.

If anything is missing, make sensible assumptions and clearly label them as “Assumptions.”

title: Turn messy research into a structured outline + first draft (with citations placeholders)
content:
I’m going to paste messy notes and source snippets below. Turn them into a publishable piece without inventing facts.

Deliverables (in this order):
1) One-sentence thesis and 3–5 key takeaways.
2) Audience + intent you infer (and one alternative angle worth considering).
3) A clean outline with H2/H3 headings. For each section, add:
   - the point it should prove
   - which note/source supports it (use [S1], [S2]…)
   - one counterpoint or limitation to mention
4) A first draft (800–1,200 words unless I specify otherwise) written in a confident, structured tone. Requirements:
   - Use short paragraphs and clear transitions
   - Define any jargon on first use
   - Include 2–3 concrete examples or mini-scenarios drawn from the notes
   - Where a claim needs support, add a citation placeholder like [S2]
   - If the notes don’t support a claim, either omit it or mark it clearly as “Unverified”
5) A “Fact-check list”: bullet the statements that should be verified, with the source tag you used or “missing source”.

Rules:
- Don’t add new statistics, quotes, or specific historical claims not present in the notes.
- If something is ambiguous, make the safest interpretation and flag the ambiguity.
- If the notes contradict each other, surface the conflict and present both sides.

Optional settings (use if provided):
- Target reader: {target_reader}
- Format: {blog post / memo / LinkedIn / script / other}
- Length: {word_count}
- Must-include points: {must_include}
- Must-avoid: {must_avoid}

Here are my notes and sources:
{paste_notes_here}

title: Dark UI-Friendly Product Hero Image for a SaaS Launch
content:
Create a premium marketing hero image for [PRODUCT/APP NAME], designed to sit inside a dark website UI.

Subject & props:
- Centerpiece: a sleek [DEVICE TYPE: phone/laptop/tablet] showing a realistic mock UI of [PRODUCT/APP NAME] (no readable brand names, use abstract UI shapes)
- Optional supporting objects: [2–3 PROPS] that hint at the product’s use-case (keep minimal, modern)

Scene & styling:
- Setting: subdued studio setup with a matte charcoal surface and a deep graphite background, minimal texture
- Color palette: cool neutrals (deep navy, graphite, gunmetal) with a restrained accent color: [ACCENT COLOR]
- Materials: anodized metal, frosted glass, soft-touch plastic; clean edges, no clutter

Composition:
- Aspect ratio: [ASPECT RATIO: 16:9 / 4:3 / 1:1]
- Camera: 50mm look, slightly above eye level, 3/4 angle, tight crop with generous negative space on [LEFT/RIGHT] for headline text
- Depth: shallow depth of field, subtle atmospheric falloff, crisp focal plane on the screen and primary edges

Lighting & mood:
- Low-key, cinematic lighting with a soft key light from [LEFT/RIGHT], gentle rim light to separate the device from background
- Soft shadows, controlled highlights, no harsh reflections, no warm daylight
- Mood: modern, direct, high-end, quiet confidence

Quality notes:
- Ultra-detailed, clean product photography style, restrained saturation, cool color grading
- No people, no busy backgrounds, no bright white environment, no cheesy stock-photo vibe

Output: one polished hero image that feels premium and readable against a dark interface.

title: Turn a Long, Messy Update Into a Clear Action-Oriented Email
content:
Rewrite the content below into a clear, practical email that busy recipients can scan in under 60 seconds.

Audience: [who this is going to]
My relationship to them: [e.g., colleague, vendor, customer]
Goal of the email: [decision? status update? request?]
Desired tone: clear, practical, calm

Constraints:
- Keep it under 220 words unless I explicitly ask for more.
- Use short paragraphs and bullets where helpful.
- Lead with the ask (or the purpose) in the first 1–2 lines.
- If there are multiple requests, group them and label each.
- Include concrete dates/times if mentioned; if missing, add a bracketed placeholder like [date].
- End with a single, specific next step and a polite sign-off.
- Remove fluff, repetition, and internal-only details.

Output format:
1) Subject line (max 8 words)
2) Email body
3) Optional: 3 alternate subject lines

Content to rewrite (can be long and messy):
[PASTE HERE]

title: Turn a feature list into a clean, developer-ready UI spec
content:
Convert the following product notes into a concise UI specification a designer and developer can build from.

Context
- Product: [what this is]
- Platform: [web / iOS / Android / desktop]
- Primary user: [who]
- Core job-to-be-done: [what they’re trying to accomplish]
- Constraints: [timeline, tech, accessibility, localization, etc.]

Input notes (paste)
[FEATURE NOTES]

Output requirements
1) Screen(s) list with one-line purpose each.
2) For each screen, include:
   - Layout structure (header/body/footer, key regions)
   - UI components (inputs, tables, cards, modals, etc.)
   - States (empty/loading/error/success/disabled)
   - Validation rules and edge cases
   - Primary/secondary actions
3) Interaction rules
   - Navigation flow between screens
   - Key micro-interactions (hover, focus, keyboard, gestures as relevant)
4) Data + content
   - Field definitions (name, type, required, constraints, example)
   - Default copy placeholders where needed
5) Acceptance criteria
   - 6–12 bullet points written as testable statements.

Style
- Use crisp bullets, no fluff.
- If something is ambiguous, make the minimum reasonable assumption and list it under “Open questions” (max 5).

title: Turn Rough Notes Into a Clear One-Page Writing Brief (with next steps)
content:
I’m going to paste messy notes for a writing project. Turn them into a clean one-page brief I can actually write from.

Notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

Output requirements:
- Keep it friendly and practical. No fluff.
- Do not invent facts; if something is missing or contradictory, flag it under “Open questions”.
- Preserve my intent and wording when it matters (names, quotes, numbers).

Please produce:
1) Project snapshot (2–3 sentences)
2) Audience + what they care about (bullets)
3) Goal of the piece + success criteria (bullets)
4) Core message (one sentence) + 3 supporting points
5) Recommended structure (H1/H2/H3 outline)
6) Key facts to verify (bullets)
7) Voice & tone guidance (3–5 bullets, including do/don’t)
8) Call to action options (3 variations)
9) Open questions (the minimum list needed to proceed)
10) First drafting step: tell me exactly what to write first (1–2 paragraphs of starter text OR a detailed opening plan—choose whichever fits best)

If the notes include multiple possible directions, suggest up to 2 angles with a quick “why this works” for each, then pick the best default and proceed with the brief.

title: Turn Product Notes Into a High-Converting Landing Page (Hero + Sections)
content:
Write landing page copy that converts based on the details below. Keep it confident, structured, and skimmable. Avoid hype, buzzwords, and vague claims—make benefits concrete.

PRODUCT NOTES
- Product/service: [what it is]
- Target audience: [who it’s for]
- Primary problem it solves: [pain]
- Key outcome/benefit: [main win]
- 3–6 features (plain words): [feature list]
- Differentiator / why choose us: [unique angle]
- Proof available (pick what’s true): [metrics, logos, testimonials, case study, years, certifications]
- Pricing/plan info (if any): [price / free trial / guarantee]
- Primary CTA: [e.g., Start free trial]
- Secondary CTA: [e.g., Book a demo]
- Brand voice constraints (optional): [e.g., no jokes, no slang]

OUTPUT
1) Hero section
- Headline (<= 10 words)
- Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
- 3 bullet benefits (outcomes, not features)
- Primary CTA + secondary CTA

2) Social proof strip
- 1 short proof line using provided proof (no inventing)

3) “How it works” section
- 3 steps with short titles + 1 sentence each

4) Benefits section
- 4 benefit cards: Title + 2 sentences each

5) Feature-to-benefit mapping
- Table with two columns: Feature | Customer impact

6) Objections & answers
- 4 common objections from this audience + crisp responses

7) Final CTA block
- 1 closing paragraph + repeated CTA buttons

Rules:
- Use specific language tied to the audience and problem.
- If any input is missing, make your best assumption but label it clearly as an assumption (don’t fabricate proof).

title: Cinematic email inbox hero image for a dark UI (modern, direct)
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image that communicates “email” without showing any readable text.

Scene: a minimalist modern desktop setup in a dim studio—matte black desk, a thin laptop slightly angled, and a floating translucent UI panel hovering above the keyboard showing an abstract inbox layout (blocks, lines, icons only; no letters, no numbers). Include a subtle envelope glyph and a small notification dot as the primary visual cue.

Art direction: modern and direct, high-end product interface vibe. Controlled low-key lighting with a cool/neutral grade (deep navy, graphite, cool whites). Soft rim light along the laptop edge, gentle specular highlights, clean shadows, and slight atmospheric falloff for depth. Background nearly black with a faint gradient and minimal bokeh.

Composition: 16:9, subject centered-left with generous negative space on the right for UI copy. Eye-level camera, 50mm look, shallow depth of field (inbox panel sharp, desk edges softly fading).

Materials: anodized aluminum laptop, matte glass UI panel, subtle reflections, no fingerprints, no clutter.

Constraints: avoid warm daylight, cozy lifestyle elements, wood-heavy tones, bright white rooms, and any legible text/logos.

Optional placeholders (apply if provided): accent color = [ACCENT_COLOR] (use sparingly for the notification dot and one UI highlight); device style = [DEVICE_STYLE] (e.g., “ultra-thin laptop” or “desktop monitor”); background tone = [BG_TONE] (e.g., “deep navy” or “charcoal”).

title: Turn a Messy Idea Into a Clear 10-Minute Action Plan
content:
Take the rough idea below and turn it into a simple action plan I can start today.

Idea (messy on purpose):
[PASTE YOUR IDEA / NOTES]

Output requirements:
- One-sentence goal (what “done” looks like)
- Assumptions (up to 3) you’re making based on my notes
- The smallest next step I can do in 10 minutes
- A 30-minute follow-up step (only if the 10-minute step goes well)
- A “stop doing” list (2–4 things to avoid that waste time)
- 3 quick checkpoints to verify I’m on track (measurable, no fluff)

Constraints:
- Keep it practical and specific; no motivational language
- If my idea is too vague, propose 2 possible interpretations and build a plan for each
- Use plain language and short bullets

title: Turn messy research into a publishable outline + first draft (with citations placeholders)
content:
I’m writing a piece and I have messy inputs. Convert them into (1) a crisp outline and (2) a solid first draft that follows the outline.

Constraints
- Tone: professional, concise, plain English.
- Audience: [AUDIENCE].
- Goal: [WHAT THIS SHOULD ACHIEVE FOR THE READER].
- Length: outline ≤ [N] headings; draft ≈ [WORD COUNT] words.
- Must avoid: hype, filler, grand claims, jargon without explanation.
- If anything is unclear or contradictory, flag it instead of guessing.

Inputs
- Topic/title idea: [TOPIC]
- Angle/thesis (if any): [THESIS OR “UNKNOWN”]
- Key points / bullet notes (raw):
  [PASTE NOTES]
- Source snippets / quotes / links (optional):
  [PASTE SOURCES]
- Required inclusions (terms, examples, constraints):
  [MUST INCLUDE]
- Exclusions / sensitive areas:
  [MUST AVOID]

Output format
A) One-paragraph premise
- 3–5 sentences: what the piece covers, why it matters, what it will *not* cover.

B) Outline
- H1/H2/H3 structure.
- For each section: 1–2 bullets explaining the point + what evidence/example supports it.
- Mark where you’re relying on assumptions with: (ASSUMPTION).

C) Draft
- Use the outline structure.
- Start with a direct lead (no anecdotes unless explicitly supported by notes).
- Every major claim must have one of:
  - a cited source placeholder like [SOURCE: …]
  - an internal support note like [FROM NOTES]
  - or be rewritten into a narrower, safer claim.
- Include at least:
  - one concrete example
  - one counterpoint + response
  - one short “So what / next steps” section

D) Gaps & questions
- List missing data, weak claims, and 5–10 targeted questions that would improve the piece fastest.

E) Optional upgrades (only if helpful)
- 3 alternate titles (non-clickbait).
- A 2-sentence executive summary.

Before you begin, ask up to 5 clarifying questions *only if* they materially change the structure or accuracy. Otherwise proceed and note assumptions.

title: Turn a Product Feature List into a Landing Page Hero + CTA Variations
content:
Take the product info below and write landing-page hero copy that’s clear, specific, and friendly.

Input:
- Product name:
- Target customer (role + industry):
- Top 3 problems they have:
- Top 5 features (bullet list):
- 1–2 key differentiators vs alternatives:
- Proof (metrics, customer logos, testimonial snippet, or “none yet”):
- Primary call to action (e.g., Start free trial, Book a demo):
- Tone constraints (optional):

Output:
1) Hero headline options (8): max 8–10 words each, no buzzword salad.
2) Subheadline options (5): 1–2 sentences, explain outcome + who it’s for.
3) Benefit bullets (6): short, concrete, start with verbs.
4) CTA button copy (10): mix of action + low-friction options.
5) Microcopy under CTA (3): reduce anxiety (time, setup, cancel anytime, privacy).
6) “How it works” (3 steps): each step 1 sentence.
7) One short testimonial draft (if proof is “none yet,” write a placeholder that doesn’t fake specifics).

Rules:
- Use plain English and specific outcomes.
- Avoid clichés like “revolutionary,” “game-changer,” “seamless,” “world-class.”
- If an input is missing, make a reasonable assumption and label it as [Assumption].
- Keep it skimmable.

Here’s the product info:
[paste here]

title: Write a confident, structured email that gets a clear decision
content:
Draft a confident, structured email based on the details below. Keep it concise, action-oriented, and easy to scan.

Context
- Recipient: [who they are / relationship]
- Goal: [what decision or outcome you want]
- Background (1–3 bullets):
  - [fact 1]
  - [fact 2]
  - [fact 3]
- Options (if any): [option A vs option B]
- Deadline / urgency: [date + why]
- Constraints: [budget, policy, dependencies]
- Your preferred path: [what you recommend]

Requirements
- Provide 3 subject line options (short, specific, no hype).
- Email structure must be:
  1) One-sentence opener with the ask
  2) 2–4 bullets of key context
  3) Clear recommendation
  4) Explicit decision request with deadline
  5) Next steps (what you’ll do once approved)
- Use confident language without sounding aggressive.
- Avoid long paragraphs; max 160 words for the email body.
- End with a simple sign-off.

Output format
1) Subject lines:
- …
- …
- …

2) Email:
[full email text]

title: Cinematic SaaS dashboard hero image with device mockup and subtle data glow
content:
Create a modern business hero image for a SaaS product.

Subject: a sleek laptop (and optional phone) showing a {PRODUCT_NAME} dashboard UI (charts, table, KPI cards) with legible but non-branded placeholder text and data.

Scene & setting: minimal studio setup in a dark premium interface-friendly environment; matte black desk surface, soft haze in the background, no clutter, no warm wood.

Composition: 3/4 angle from slightly above, device centered-left with negative space on the right for headline copy; shallow depth of field; clean leading lines toward the screen.

Lighting: low-key, directional key light from upper left + subtle rim light on device edges; soft shadows; controlled reflections on the screen (no blown highlights).

Color & mood: cool/neutral grading (deep navy, charcoal, cool gray), restrained saturation, subtle cyan/blue data glow accents; calm, confident, high-end.

Style: photorealistic product photography with a cinematic feel; crisp materials (anodized aluminum, glass), fine texture detail.

Constraints: no people, no logos, no recognizable brands, no bright daylight, no busy office background.

Optional variations: include a floating translucent analytics overlay (thin lines, subtle glow) around the screen; add a faint grid pattern in the background.

Output: ultra-high resolution, sharp focus on device and UI, premium commercial look.

title: LLM Writing Intake Router: classify requests and output the right workflow
content:
Build an intake-and-routing system for writing-related requests. Your output must be an operator-usable routing artifact (not a polished article/email).

Input I will provide each time:
- USER_REQUEST: the raw text from the user
- (optional) CONTEXT: audience, channel, constraints, examples, brand voice, deadlines

Your job:
1) Classify the request into exactly ONE primary route.
2) Produce the workflow steps + required fields for that route.
3) Return a structured “work order” the operator (or downstream agent) can execute.
4) If information is missing, ask only the minimum high-impact questions (max 5) and still produce a provisional work order with assumptions clearly marked.

Routes (choose one):
A) Draft from scratch (new content)
B) Rewrite / reframe (existing text provided)
C) Shorten / expand to target length
D) Tone & voice alignment (brand/persona constraints)
E) Structural edit (outline, flow, headings, argument)
F) Copyediting (grammar, clarity, consistency)
G) Fact-check & citation plan (verification workflow, not actual browsing)
H) Multi-asset repurpose (one source → many formats)
I) Prompt creation (meta: writing prompts, templates, macros)
J) Other writing-adjacent (explain why it doesn’t fit above)

Routing rules:
- If USER_REQUEST includes existing text to transform, prefer B/F/E depending on goal.
- If the user asks for “make it shorter/longer,” prefer C unless they also request tone or structure as primary.
- If the user wants multiple deliverables (tweet thread + blog + email), choose H.
- If the user explicitly wants a reusable prompt/template, choose I.
- If the user emphasizes correctness, sources, or claims, choose G.

Output format (strict JSON):
{
  "route": "A|B|C|D|E|F|G|H|I|J",
  "confidence": 0.0,
  "reason": "1-2 sentences",
  "inputs_needed": {
    "required": ["..."],
    "optional": ["..."],
    "detected_from_user": {"field": "value"}
  },
  "clarifying_questions": ["... up to 5"],
  "assumptions": ["..."],
  "workflow": [
    {"step": 1, "name": "...", "instructions": "...", "checks": ["..."]}
  ],
  "deliverable_spec": {
    "format": "e.g., outline, edited text with track-changes markers, headline set, content matrix",
    "length_or_limits": "...",
    "voice": "...",
    "structure": "...",
    "acceptance_criteria": ["..."]
  },
  "handoff": {
    "to_downstream_agent": "what to do next",
    "paste_ready_prompt": "A single prompt that can be sent to a writing agent to execute this work order. Keep it under 1800 characters."
  }
}

Quality checks (apply to every run):
- Ensure the chosen route is singular and defensible.
- Make acceptance criteria testable (e.g., word count range, reading level, must-include points).
- If safety/legal/medical claims appear, add a check step for risk flags and suggest disclaimers as a workflow step (do not write the full disclaimer text).

Now wait for my USER_REQUEST and CONTEXT.
